abstract: This study offers a comprehensive evaluation of contemporary leadership practices within the Armed Forces of Bosnia and Herzegovina. By surveying 200 service members, the research descriptively assesses the prevalence of motivational leadership, the nature of the military environment, the core characteristics of military leaders, and the resulting organizational benefits. The descriptive analysis reveals a high level of consensus among respondents, with results alignining closely with theoretical expectations. Specifically, finding indicate strong positive agreement regarding the professional characteristics of leaders and the broader advantages of effective military leadership. Furthermore, the results highlight a positive motivational climate and an environment characterized by routine activities and standardized missions—a finding consistent with the structured nature of military service. Ultimately, the study concludes that the surveyed forces employ a multifaceted leadership approach that is task, team, and organization-oriented, while increasingly incorporating elements of shared leadership.
